title: Duration-Based Valuation of Corporate Bonds
abstract: We decompose corporate bond and equity index returns into duration-matched government bond returns and the excess returns over this duration-matched counterfactual, which we term duration-adjusted returns. Compared with previously used excess return definitions (ie, returns in excess of Treasury bills), our decomposition leads to markedly different return patterns and asset pricing implications. In particular, we find that investment-grade bonds earn a small credit risk premium, comparable in magnitude to the convenience yield, and that duration adjustment resolves the CAPM’s failure to price corporate bonds. These findings highlight the importance of adjusting for nonstationary interest rate environments in asset pricing tests.

author.Jules H van Binsbergen*: The Wharton School, University of Pennsylvania,United States

Over the past five decades, investors have witnessed a secular decline in discount rates (or expected returns) on a number of asset classes, including real bonds, nominal bonds and equities. This secular decline in rates has, ceteris paribus, led to large valuation windfalls, particularly for long-duration assets, such as long maturity bonds and equities.1These ex post positive return realizations complicate the evaluation of asset pricing models that make predictions about ex ante expected returns. That is, in nonstationary environments, ex ante expectations and ex post realizations may not coincide.

This paper studies the cross-sectional asset pricing implications of the secular decline in interest rates using the corporate bond market as a laboratory. To correct for the effect of declining interest rates, we decompose individual corporate bond returns into duration-matched government bond returns and the excess return over and above this duration-matched counterfactual. This decomposition leads us to a number of findings on the drivers of corporate bond returns that differ from the existing literature that primarily uses a standard definition of excess returns relative to the Treasury-bill rate.

We begin by showing that the majority of the excess returns of corporate bonds relative to Treasury bills from 1986 to 2020 is attributable to gains on long-term Treasury securities rather than compensation for credit and liquidity risk. We find that these risks account for only 9% (37%) of standard excess returns for investment-grade (high-yield) corporate bonds, while the long-term Treasury return accounts for the remaining 91% (63%). This echoes the results on the stock and bond markets inVan Binsbergen (2021). The implication of this finding is that existing studies that conduct asset pricing tests on excess returns relative to Treasury bills are primarily studying the returns to long-term risk-free assets, rather than the returns to credit and liquidity risks that are specific to the corporate bond market.

Another finding that stems from our decomposition of corporate bond returns is that the realized risk premium for bearing credit and liquidity risks from 1986 to 2020 is small in magnitude. The average duration-adjusted excess return of investment-grade (high-yield) corporate bonds over this period is 0.38% (1.94%) annualized. Both of these estimates are statistically indistinguishable from zero. The investment-grade credit risk premium is similar in magnitude to estimates of the convenience yield in safe asset markets (e.g.,Van Binsbergen, Diamond, and Grotteria 2022). These risk premium estimates are lower than the average credit spreads of 1.36% (5.16%) due to defaults, issuers’ exercise of embedded options to prepay bonds, and widening credit spreads for bonds outstanding at the end of the sample period. Ultimately, asset pricing regressions inform us as to whether the return to bearing credit and liquidity risk is high, as argued in prior literature (e.g.,Huang and Huang 2012on the “credit spread puzzle”), or low.

Along these lines, we show that the failure to adjust returns for duration exposure plays an important role in the inability of the capital asset pricing model (CAPM) and other equity factor models to price corporate bonds. Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) show that existing multifactor equity and bond models are unable to fit the standard excess returns of corporate bond portfolios sorted by downside risk.2Sorting by credit rating, we find similar evidence regarding the CAPM and corporate bond excess returns relative to Treasury bills, with positive and statistically significant regression intercepts andR2coefficients near zero for investment-grade bonds. It is worth noting that these findings contrast with the early literature on corporate bonds, which found no evidence of mispricing under the CAPM and similar models (e.g.,Blume and Keim 1987;Fama and French 1993;Elton, Gruber, and Blake 1995). However, the sample periods in these papers differ, with Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) examining data from 2002 to 2016, whileBlume and Keim (1987)study 1977 to 1986,Fama and French (1993)study 1963 to 1991, andElton, Gruber, and Blake (1995)study 1980 to 1992.

Although the CAPM is unable to explain variation in the standard excess returns of corporate bond portfolios, it has significant explanatory power and statistically insignificant intercepts when applied to the duration-adjusted excess returns, which are driven by credit and liquidity risk rather than changes in the term structure of Treasury yields. The implication, which we support directly, is that the inability of the CAPM to price corporate bonds is due to the confounding effects of unexpectedly high realized returns on long-term government debt, rather than an inability to price credit and liquidity risks.

This could explain the different evidence on mispricing under the CAPM between Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) and the early literature. Long-term Treasury yields fluctuated over the samples covered in the early literature but did not exhibit a significant trend, whereas the recent period studied in Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) and this paper is characterized by steadily declining interest rates. Thus, it is likely that duration plays a larger role recently than it did in earlier research. It is also likely that the changing correlation between stocks and Treasury returns (Campbell, Pflueger, and Viceira 2020) has an effect on these asset pricing tests. Indeed, when we split the sample into the 1986–2000 and 2001–2020 subperiods, when the stock-bond correlation is positive and negative, respectively, we find that the CAPM’s failure to price corporate bonds is concentrated in the latter period.

Next, we take the intuition from the decomposition of test asset returns to the factor returns, using the equity index return decomposition fromVan Binsbergen (2021)to form a “duration-adjusted CAPM” based on the duration-matched Treasury return and the return to bearing dividend risk. This two-factor model based on equity returns fits the standard excess returns of corporate bonds significantly better than the single-factor CAPM because of its ability to capture the duration component of corporate bond returns. This model is similar in spirit to the TERM and DEF factors inFama and French (1993), but has the benefit of not needing a separate set of bond market factors. Instead, it is an integrated model of bond and stock markets, which nests the CAPM because the factors sum to the market return. Finally, to assess the importance of market segmentation we construct a two-factor model based on the duration-matched Treasury return and duration-adjusted return components of the corporate bond market return, which we call the “duration-adjusted bond CAPM.”

We compare the performance of the CAPM, the duration-adjusted CAPM, and the duration-adjusted bond CAPM to the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model, which has four factors including the corporate bond market return and downside risk, credit risk, and liquidity risk factors based on cross-sectional portfolio sorts.

We find that the duration-adjusted CAPM has weaker explanatory power for standard excess returns than the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model. However, despite having two fewer factors, the duration-adjusted bond CAPM performs significantly better at explaining the time series of corporate bond portfolio returns and is similarly able to explain the cross-section of returns.

Even the simple CAPM significantly outperforms the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model when applied to the duration-adjusted excess returns of corporate bonds. The inclusion of corporate bond market returns in the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model appears to give it better explanatory power for the duration-matched Treasury component and therefore the excess returns of corporate bonds relative to Treasury bills. However, the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model underperforms the CAPM at explaining the return to bearing credit risk (ie, duration-adjusted returns), despite the paper’s focus on constructing factors for downside and credit risk. This highlights the importance of disentangling the effects of duration to truly understand what drives the performance of asset pricing models. Since the duration-adjusted CAPM has an extra degree of freedom and the duration-adjusted bond CAPM has the added benefit of information specific to the corporate bond market, these models naturally perform even better than the CAPM at explaining duration-adjusted corporate bond returns.

Our main analysis focuses on portfolios sorted by credit rating category, because this method of sorting has intuitive connections with both duration (longer for higher-rated bonds) and credit risk (higher for lower-rated bonds). We extend the set of characteristics to classify corporate bonds and examine the portfolios sorted by credit spreads, downside risk, illiquidity and equity momentum. For credit spreads, downside risk and illiquidity, the duration-adjusted CAPM significantly reduces alphas from the CAPM and generates insignificant alphas for long-short portfolios. However, the duration-adjusted CAPM has no explanatory power for the momentum effect, suggesting that not all anomalies are created equal: Some reflect the decline in interest rates in recent decades, while others do not. In addition, to ensure that our findings are not driven by this choice of portfolio construction, inInternet Appendix Table A1we show that our main results are robust to using portfolios sorted by the issuer’s industry.

This paper contributes to several strands of literature. Most directly, we build on the recent literature exploring the role of duration in driving equity returns in the time series (Van Binsbergen et al. 2013)and the cross-section (Weber 2018;Gonçalves 2021;Gormsen and Lazarus 2023). In particular,Van Binsbergen (2021)decomposes equity index returns into duration-matched Treasury returns and duration-adjusted equity returns. He finds that much of the average returns and volatility of the equity index corresponds to the Treasury component rather than the dividend component. Our paper is unique in its focus on corporate bonds, which have well defined cash flows that allow a more precise estimation of duration-matched Treasury returns than is possible in the equity market. We use this setting to demonstrate that the steady decline in long-term interest rates over the past four decades has a material impact on the interpretation of asset pricing tests that use the standard definition of excess returns relative to Treasury bills.

Our results on the ability of the CAPM and duration-adjusted CAPM to price corporate bonds are related to prior research on the integration of corporate debt and equity markets. In theory, the one-factor assumption ofMerton (1974)leads to integration of the two markets, suggesting that the equity return-based model should price corporate bonds. However, the empirical literature is mixed, with some papers finding market segmentation in within-firm (e.g.,Kwan 1996;Choi and Kim 2018;Lewis 2019;Sandulescu 2020)and market-level evidence (e.g.,Collin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Martin 2001;Lettau, Maggiori, and Weber 2014;Collin-Dufresne, Junge, and Trolle 2024), while others find evidence of market integration (e.g.,Fama and French 1993;Chen, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein 2009;Culp, Nozawa, and Veronesi 2018;Elkamhi, Jo, and Nozawa 2023). We shed light on this issue by showing that the ability of equity factor models to price corporate bonds is significantly improved by making a simple duration adjustment to either the bonds or the factors. While this set of results could be viewed as supporting market integration, our finding that the duration-adjusted bond CAPM, based on corporate bond market returns, outperforms the equity-based models suggests a degree of segmentation.

We also contribute to the rapidly growing literature on corporate bond pricing. Early papers in this literature describe the risk exposures of corporate bonds (e.g.,Blume, Keim, and Patel 1991;Fama and French 1993;Elton, Gruber, and Blake 1995) and the roles of default risk (Gebhardt, Hvidkjaer, and Swaminathan 2005a) and liquidity risk (Lin, Wang, and Wu 2011) in driving returns. More recently, several papers have introduced multifactor models based on bond characteristics and bond portfolio sorts (e.g.,Israel, Palhares, and Richardson 2018; Bai, Bali, and Wen 2019;Bredendiek, Ottonello, and Valkanov 2019;Chung, Wang, and Wu 2019; Bali, Subrahmanyam, and Wen 2021;He, Khorrami, and Song 2022;Kelly, Palhares, and Pruitt 2023;Bartram, Grinblatt, and Nozawa 2023). Finally,Dickerson, Mueller, and Robotti (2023)andDickerson, Robotti, and Rossetti (2023)compare the performance of various bond-factor models and conclude that the bond market factor is preferred as it has a relatively high Sharpe ratio. Most of these papers use the standard excess return definition relative to Treasury bills, which we show is dominated by Treasury returns over the past few decades, especially in the investment-grade segment.3Given that the performance of long-term Treasuries is unlikely to repeat itself because nominal rates approached the zero lower bound at the end of our sample period, the out-of-sample applicability of these models may be limited. We show that simple two-factor models based on the duration-matched Treasury and duration-adjusted returns of equity or bonds have significant explanatory power without adding more complex factors.
We compute corporate bond returns from 1986 to 2020 using price quotes from two sources. From January 1986 to June 1997, we use the Lehman/Warga Fixed Income database, which provides monthly quotes back to 1973. We restrict attention to bonds that are actively quoted, dropping matrix prices. We begin the sample in 1986, which is the first year with at least 100 high-yield bonds per month in the sample. This cutoff aligns withEom, Helwege, and Huang (2004)and uses 2 more years of data thanCollin-Dufresne, Goldstein, and Martin (2001), but shortens the sample period relative toGilchrist and Zakrajsek (2012)andNozawa (2017), who include matrix pricing in their analyses.

From July 1997 to December 2020, we use daily quote data from Bank of America Merrill Lynch (BAML) that form the basis of BAML’s corporate bond indices. We take the last quote of each month to extend the bond-month panel. Prior academic research using the BAML data includesSchaefer and Strebulaev (2008),Feldhütter and Schaefer (2018), andSchwert (2020). The BAML data include fewer financial issuers than the Lehman/Warga data, so to ensure uniformity across time, we exclude issuers in the financial (SIC between 6000 and 6999), utility (SIC 4900–4999), and quasi-public industries (SIC above 8999) from the sample. This industry restriction is consistent with standard practice in the corporate finance literature. When the two data sets are combined, the panel data span 419 months, 1 month short of 35 years because there is no valid bond price in April 1988.

We restrict the sample to bonds that are denominated in U.S. dollars, senior secured or senior unsecured in priority, nonconvertible, and pay fixed, semiannual coupon payments in cash (ie, not payment-in-kind). Following the literature, we exclude bonds with less than 1 year to maturity. Finally, we exclude a small number of bonds with maturity over 30 years to avoid the need to extrapolate the Treasury yield curve for the bond return decomposition described below.

Our sample covers a large fraction of the U.S. corporate bond universe but is tilted toward larger and more liquid issues. Thus, it is worthwhile to establish that our data are representative of the overall market. First,Internet Appendix Figure A1shows that value-weighted portfolios of investment-grade and high-yield bonds in our sample closely track the corresponding Bloomberg-Barclays indices with correlation coefficients of 0.98. Second, our findings are robust to using the Trade Compliance and Reporting Engine (TRACE) Enhanced database, which contains historical transactions in the secondary market for corporate bonds.Internet Appendix A2provides the paper’s main results using TRACE data.

1.1 Decomposition of bond returns

The return of a corporate bond is: r i , t = P i , t + A I i , t + C i , t P i , t − 1 + A I i , t − 1 − 1 , (1) where P i , t is the clean bid price of bond i at the end of period t , A I i , t is the accrued interest, and C i , t is the semiannual coupon payment if period t is a coupon payment period. 8 We assume that bonds in default according to the Mergent Fixed Income Securities Database (FISD), or within 90 days of default and trading below a clean price of 40, are not making coupon payments (ie, trading “flat”). This choice reflects the standard treatment of unsecured bonds in bankruptcy as well as the clawback of preference payments within 90 days of a Chapter 11 filing, and affects less than 0.2% of bond-month observations in our sample.

Panel B ofTable 1presents statistics on the monthly returns of corporate bonds sorted into value-weighted portfolios by credit rating category. Interestingly, the arithmetic average returns are similar across rating categories, with slightly lower returns for the B-rated bonds. Consistent with their lower default risk, the standard deviation of returns is lower for bonds with better credit ratings.

Annualizing the returns in panel B and comparing them to the yields in panel A ofTable 1leads to an interesting pattern: investment-grade bonds earned higher average returns (7.32%) than their average promised yields (6.00%) over the sample period. This is counterintuitive at first glance, but natural when one considers how declining rates affect bond prices and the fact that not all bonds mature in the sample period, the effect of which we examine below. The opposite pattern appears for high-yield bonds, which is natural given their higher rate of default.

Our analysis centers on the definition of excess returns relative to a duration-matched benchmark instead of the short-term risk-free rate. We construct the duration-matched Treasury return using the concept of Macaulay duration. Macaulay duration is commonly computed as the weighted average time it takes for asset i to return the discounted cash flows to its owner: D u r i , t = ∑ k = 1 ∞ w i , t , k t k , (2) where w i , t , k is the weight that the present value of the k th cash flow has in the asset value and t k is the time until the k th cash flow. For a corporate bond with semiannual coupon payments and $100 face value, the Macaulay duration is defined as: D u r i , t = 1 2 P i , t [ ∑ k = 1 K ( t ^ k × C i 2 ( 1 + y i , t 2 ) t ^ k ) + t ^ K × 100 ( 1 + y i , t 2 ) t ^ K ] , (3) where P i , t is the all-in price of the bond (ie, including accrued interest), C i is the annual coupon rate, y i , t is the yield to maturity, k indexes the semiannual coupon periods, K denotes the terminal period, and t ^ k is the number of semiannual coupon periods from the present date t until the k th cash flow under the 30/360 daycount convention. Each term in the summation maps into a weight w i , t , k in Equation (2) . 9

After determining the duration-based weighting of the cash flows for each bond-month observation, we compute the duration-matched Treasury return as: r i , t + 1 T s y = ∑ k = 1 K w i , t , k r b , t + 1 , k . (4)

The monthly return on the k -period zero-coupon Treasury bond is given by: r b , t + 1 , k = e x p ( − t k − 1 y b , t + 1 , k − 1 ) e x p ( − t k y b , t , k ) − 1 , (5) where y b , t + 1 , k is the continuously compounded yield based on the term structure parameters from Gürkaynak, Sack, and Wright (2007) and t k is the actual time until the cash flow k in years.

Finally, we define the duration-adjusted return as the difference between a bond’s actual return and its duration-matched risk-free return: r i , t + 1 D u r A d j = r i , t + 1 − r i , t + 1 T s y . (6)

InInternet Appendix A1 and Table A2, we consider alternative weighting schemes: first, we match cash flow maturity (rather than duration), and second, we estimate the weightwi,t,kby regressing corporate bond returns on Treasury returns. These alternative estimates lead to qualitatively similar but subtly different results which provide intuitive support for our preferred weighting scheme. Specifically, the maturity-based weighting scheme overstates the role of interest rate risk in corporate bond returns by placing more weight on long-maturity cash flows than fixed-income pricing theory would suggest. The regression-based approach understates the role of interest rate risk, likely due to the negative correlation between interest rates and credit spreads, which attenuates the sensitivity of corporate bond returns to Treasury returns.

Figure 2presents the decomposed returns of value-weighted portfolios of investment-grade and high-yield bonds to illustrate the relative contributions of duration and risk to the overall return. Panel A shows that investment-grade bond returns barely exceed the returns of duration-matched Treasury securities. Only since the financial crisis has risk provided a positive contribution to the overall return of investment-grade bonds. Panel B shows that intuitively, the duration-adjusted return plays a more prominent role in the overall performance of high-yield bonds, contributing to both the upside and the downside. Because of default losses in the 2001 and 2007–2009 recessions, the total return of high-yield bonds has only recently exceeded the duration-matched Treasury return.

For both investment-grade and high-yield bonds, the duration-matched Treasury and duration-adjusted returns are negatively correlated with coefficients around –0.5. This correlation intuitively reflects the fact that credit spreads tend to rise when risk-free rates are falling and vice versa (Longstaff and Schwartz 1995). The duration-matched Treasury return is positively correlated with total returns for investment-grade bonds (⁠ρ= 0.71) but has little correlation with total returns for high-yield bonds (⁠ρ= 0.04). Duration-adjusted returns are positively correlated with total returns for both categories, but the correlation coefficient is higher for high-yield bonds (⁠ρ= 0.84) than investment-grade bonds (⁠ρ= 0.27).

1.2 Decomposition of equity index returns

In addition to pricing the decomposed bond returns with existing factor models, we examine the ability of the CAPM to explain corporate bond returns when the effect of duration is stripped out of the equity market return. Due to the uncertain nature of equity dividends, in contrast to the fixed payments of corporate bonds, more structure is necessary to perform the equity index decomposition.

We implement the strip-by-strip matching based on the Gordon growth equation for the stock market index in continuous time. Let the continuously compounded expected return and dividend growth rate be μ and g , respectively. Denote the dividend of the index at time t by D t . The Gordon growth equation expresses the value of the index as follows: S t = D t ∫ 0 ∞ e ( g − μ ) τ d τ = D t μ − g , (7)

We can rearrange Equation (7) to show that the dividend yield is equal to the difference between its expected return and dividend growth rate: D t S t = μ − g . (8)

The present value of the m -th dividend strip is given by: P t , m = D t e ( g − μ ) m . (9)

This implies a weighting scheme for the m -th dividend strip value as w t , m = P t , m S t = ( μ − g ) e ( g − μ ) m . (10)

Following Van Binsbergen (2021) , we use the concept of Macaulay Duration (Dur) to characterize the duration of the stock index: D u r t = ∫ 0 ∞ w t , m m d m , = ∫ 0 ∞ ( μ − g ) e ( g − μ ) m m d m , = 1 μ − g , (11) which shows that under the Gordon growth assumptions, the duration is equal to the inverse of the index dividend yield μ − g ⁠ .

Given our focus on monthly bond portfolio returns, we need a monthly weighting scheme. To this end, we convert the continuous-time weighting scheme in Equation (10) to a monthly weighting scheme as follows: w t , n = ∫ n − 1 n w t , m d m , = ∫ n − 1 n ( μ − g ) e ( g − μ ) m d m , = e ( g − μ ) ( n − 1 ) − e ( g − μ ) n , (12) which gives the weighting scheme for a n -th monthly dividend strip.

The duration-matched Treasury return r T s y , t + 1 can be calculated as: r T s y , t + 1 = ∑ k = 1 ∞ w t , k r b , t + 1 , k , (13) where the weights are calculated using Equation (12) .

As in Van Binsbergen (2021) , we use a time-varying weighting scheme. Specifically, in each month, we calculate the dividend yield in the data as d y t = ∑ n = t − 11 t d n S t , (14) where d n is the dividend in month n .

Based onEquation (8), we use this current dividend yield as the input forμ−ginEquation (12)to obtain the weights. We employ a cutoff of 30 years (360 months) for the term structure data of government bonds and assign the residual weight to the terminal period, followingVan Binsbergen (2021). For example, if 40% of the portfolio value comes from dividends paid in year 30 and beyond, then the 30-year Treasury strip receives a weight of 40% in the counterfactual portfolio.

Once we obtain duration-matched Treasury returns, r T s y , t + 1 ⁠ , from Equation (13) , we can calculate the duration-adjusted stock return: r D u r A d j , t + 1 = r S P 500 , t + 1 − r T s y , t + 1 . (15)

Table 1, panel C, summarizes the return decomposition of the S&P 500 Index, which we use as a proxy for the equity market, as well as other factor series we use in the subsequent analysis. Consistent withVan Binsbergen (2021), the duration-matched government bond return accounts for nearly all of the equity index return over the sample period. The duration-adjusted return, subtracting the duration-matched Treasury return from the total return, is close to zero on average but has a higher variance than the duration-matched Treasury return. The total return has correlation coefficients of -0.08 with the duration-matched Treasury return and 0.67 with the duration-adjusted return, while the two components of the total return are strongly negatively correlated with a coefficient of –0.79.
article.2 Results:
2.1 Decomposed corporate bond returns

Table 2summarizes the decomposed corporate bond returns. Panel A reports average monthly returns for value-weighted portfolios of corporate bonds sorted by the credit rating at the end of the prior month. The first three columns report the raw return, the 1-month Treasury-bill rate, and the standard excess return of these portfolios. Average monthly total and excess returns are generally increasing in credit risk, but the difference between investment-grade (IG) and high-yield (HY) bonds is only 0.084% per month (or 1.01% per year). The average excess return is equivalent to 58% (63%) of the total return for investment-grade (high-yield) bonds, which would typically be interpreted as the compensation to investors for bearing credit and liquidity risk. Unreportedt-tests indicate that the average excess return is statistically significant at the 1% level for all of the portfolios presented here.

The sample consists of 419 monthly observations from January 1986 to December 2020. Panel A is based on portfolios rebalanced each month; panel B is based on portfolios sorted by the bond’s initial rating and not rebalanced; panel C splits the sample by exit path, that is, matured, called, defaulted, or censored (still outstanding at the end of 2020); and panel D splits the sample by the presence of embedded options, that is, noncallable bonds, bonds with a fixed-price call schedule, and bonds without a fixed-price call but with a make-whole call provision. From left to right, the columns contain the average monthly return for each corporate bond portfolio; the 1-month Treasury-bill return and the standard excess corporate bond return; the duration-matched Treasury return and the duration-adjusted excess corporate bond return; and the log duration-adjusted excess corporate bond return (to account for compounding). In panels C and D, the rightmost column reports the average fraction of bonds in each subsample.

The next three columns consider the duration-adjusted approach to computing excess returns. This approach leads to nearly double the difference, 0.13% monthly (1.56% per year) between investment-grade and high-yield bond returns. In stark contrast to the standard approach to measuring excess returns, risk compensation accounts for only 5% (23%) of the total return after taking out the duration-matched Treasury return. In other words, long-term Treasury returns account for 91% (63%) of corporate bonds’ excess returns under the standard approach.10Moreover, the estimated risk premium of each portfolio after duration adjustment is statistically indistinguishable from zero.

Given the low contribution of credit and liquidity risk, a natural question is whether rating transitions are biasing our return estimates (e.g., high-rated bonds that are downgraded move to a lower portfolio but long-term investors still earn a good return). To address this concern, panel B ofTable 2presents the same decomposition with portfolios sorted by the bond’s initial rating (ie, no transitions due to upgrades or downgrades). Perhaps unsurprisingly, the duration-adjusted returns of investment-grade (high-yield) bonds increase (decrease), since the returns of fallen angels that do not ultimately default are shifted to the investment-grade portfolio. However, this panel reveals the same qualitative conclusions, with risk compensation accounting for only 8% (19%) of the total IG (HY) return.

There is a noticeable difference between credit spreads and duration-adjusted returns over our sample period.Table 1shows that the average IG (HY) credit spread (without option adjustment) is 1.36% (5.16%) over duration-matched Treasury yields, equivalent to 23% (53%) of the yield-to-maturity.

What is driving this difference? There are three possible sources: default, prepayment, and censoring in the data (e.g., because of the sample ending). In the absence of these events, the realized return of a bond should equal its yield-to-maturity.

Panel C ofTable 2examines this issue by splitting the sample by the outcome for each bond. The first two rows show that investment-grade bonds that matured had higher duration-adjusted returns than bonds that were called, defaulted, or were still outstanding at the end of the sample. The monthly premium earned by matured bonds is equivalent to 0.60% annualized, or about 10% of the total return. In contrast, investment-grade bonds that were called early earned returns that were only 0.003% per month higher than duration-matched Treasuries, due to the widening of credit spreads as interest rates fall (Longstaff and Schwartz 1995). Naturally, bonds that defaulted had large negative average returns, while bonds that were censored by the end of the sample had slightly lower returns than the full sample average.11

One issue with the analysis in panel C is that it sorts on an ex post outcome that is not in investors’ information set at the time of portfolio formation. Panel D addresses this issue by splitting the sample on the presence of embedded options, an ex ante sorting variable that is observable by investors. In the investment-grade (high-yield) segment, the returns of noncallable bonds are 0.05% (1.18%) higher annualized than the returns of bonds with a fixed-price call schedule. In tandem with the ex post sample split, this suggests that persistent declines in long-term interest rates have transferred value from investors to issuers in the set of high-yield bonds that issuers can prepay when conditions are favorable.

Overall, a few key themes emerge fromTable 2. First, the standard approach to calculating excess returns does a poor job measuring risk compensation in the corporate bond market. The vast majority of realized excess returns from 1986 to 2020 are due to Treasury returns rather than credit and liquidity risks that are specific to corporate bonds. Second, investor compensation for bearing credit and liquidity risk over this period is small in economic magnitude and accounts for a small fraction of overall returns, especially for investment-grade bonds. Finally, these low returns are driven in part by embedded options allowing issuers to call debt before maturity, which are most likely to be exercised when interest rates decline after issuance. This effect is observed both in bonds that are called and in callable bonds that have not yet been prepaid.

2.2 Failure of equity factors to price corporate bonds

To demonstrate the importance of adjusting for duration when evaluating asset pricing models, we assess the ability of the CAPM, originally derived bySharpe (1964), to price corporate bonds. We focus on the CAPM because it is the simplest equity factor model. While we do not claim that the CAPM is the best model for corporate bonds, its simplicity helps clarify our contribution to studying the impact of duration adjustment in asset pricing tests. However, our qualitative conclusions are similar if additional equity market factors are added to the pricing model.

This analysis starts with bond portfolios sorted by credit rating, which were summarized inTable 1, because these have intuitive associations with credit risk and duration. In Section 2.4, we present results based on portfolios sorted by other characteristics.

Panel A shows that the CAPM is unable to price corporate bonds, particularly in the investment-grade rating categories. The intercepts are statistically significant at the 5% level for every investment-grade portfolio and economically large, between 0.26% and 0.31% per month. Strikingly, the adjustedR2coefficient is less than 5% for bonds rated AA and A, and less than 15% for BBB-rated bonds. The model fits better for high-yield bonds, withR2coefficients exceeding 30% and a statistically insignificant intercept for the B category, consistent with lower-rated bonds having more exposure to economic conditions. TheGibbons, Ross, and Shanken (1989)(GRS) test rejects the hypothesis that the intercepts are jointly zero at the 0.1% level of significance.

Panels B and C shed light on this finding by estimating the CAPM regressions for the duration-matched Treasury returns and duration-adjusted returns defined inEquations (4)and(6), respectively. The CAPM is unable to price the duration-matched Treasury returns, with statistically significant intercepts across the rating spectrum andR2coefficients near zero. Similar to the total returns, the GRS test rejects the hypothesis that the intercepts are jointly zero.

However, the CAPM does not exhibit the same failure for the duration-adjusted returns, with statistically insignificant intercepts in all categories and a GRS testp-value of 0.36, failing to reject that the intercepts are jointly equal to zero. The adjustedR2coefficient shows substantial improvement for the investment-grade categories. Intuitively, the market beta coefficients are higher for lower-rated bonds with higher default risk.

This finding is not specific to the CAPM. In theInternet Appendix Tables A4 and A5, we show that the same conclusion holds when we use the five-factor model ofFama and French (2015)and the four-factor model ofHou, Xue, and Zhang (2015). That is, the equity return-based model cannot price the average corporate bond returns in excess of T-bills, but they do explain the average duration-adjusted returns.

Overall, the results inTable 3suggest that the failure of equity factor models to price corporate bonds is largely due to their inability to price the duration-matched government bond returns. Indeed, the equity market return has substantial explanatory power for the duration-adjusted returns, which are driven by credit risk and liquidity.

2.3 The role of the equity Treasury correlation

An important development over our sample period is the shift in the correlation between equity market and Treasury returns from positive to negative around the year 2000 (Campbell, Pflueger, and Viceira 2020). Since Treasury returns account for a large share of corporate bond returns, especially for the investment-grade segment, this shift could have an important effect on the ability of the CAPM to price corporate bonds.

To explore this issue,Table 4reports estimates ofEquation (16)for the 1986–2000 and 2001–2020 subperiods. Panels A1 and A2, which are based on standard excess returns relative to Treasury bills, reveal the effects of the changing stock-bond correlation. From 1986 to 2000, when equity and Treasury returns were positively correlated, the CAPM has nontrivial explanatory power for investment-grade bonds. Though the regression intercepts are economically significant, they are not statistically significant. In contrast, from 2001 to 2020, we observe similar estimates toTable 3, with highly significant intercepts and near-zero beta loadings andR2coefficients for investment-grade bonds. When Treasury and equity returns are positively correlated, corporate bond returns are correlated with stock market returns; when the equity-Treasury correlation is negative, corporate bond and stock market returns are effectively uncorrelated.

Panels B1 and B2, which are based on duration-adjusted returns, reveal additional differences across subperiods. In contrast to the results on standard excess returns, the CAPM is unable to price duration-adjusted returns from 1986 to 2000. Although the regression intercepts are statistically insignificant, the beta loadings are statistically insignificant and theR2coefficients are approximately zero. From 2001 to 2020, the CAPM has relative success similar toTable 3, with statistically insignificant intercepts near zero, beta loadings that increase in fundamental risk, andR2coefficients above 30% for all rating categories.

Table 4builds onTable 3by emphasizing the challenge of disentangling the roles of discount rates and risk premiums in driving the returns of risky bonds and equities. Depending on the correlation between Treasury and equity returns, the CAPM is unable to explain corporate bond excess returns relative to Treasury bills (2001–2020) or it is unable to explain duration-adjusted returns (1986–2000). In the next section, we consider an alternative framework that addresses this issue.

2.4 Duration-adjusted capital asset pricing model

The results in Tables 3 and 4 suggest a natural extension of the CAPM into duration-matched Treasury and dividend risk components that should capture these distinct risks in the corporate bond portfolios. Using the equity index decomposition described in Section 1.2, define the duration-adjusted CAPM as: r i , t − r f , t = α + β T s y ( r T s y , t − r f , t ) + β D u r A d j ( r S P 500 , t − r T s y , t ) + ε i , t , (17) where r T s y , t is the return of the Treasury portfolio that matches the duration of the S&P 500 Index from Van Binsbergen (2021) and r S P 500 , t − r T s y , t represents the equity index return in excess of the duration-driven return.

Table 5presents estimates of the duration-adjusted CAPM for the corporate bond portfolio returns as well as their components. Panel A reveals a remarkable improvement in the ability to explain corporate bond returns, especially at the high end of the credit quality spectrum. The intercepts for investment-grade bonds are statistically insignificant and theR2coefficients are between 41% and 63%. The model’s performance is less improved for the high-yield bonds, but the average absolute alphas shrink to 0.14% from the standard CAPM’s alpha of 0.26%

As before, panels B and C use the bond return decomposition to show where the improvement comes from. Panel B shows that the duration-matched Treasury return for the equity market is able to price the duration-matched Treasury returns for corporate bonds, withR2coefficients between 69% and 85%. Panel C shows that the duration-adjusted CAPM explains a larger proportion of the duration-adjusted corporate bond returns than the CAPM, with increases inR2exceeding 16 percentage points (p.p.) across the rating spectrum. The GRS test fails to reject the hypothesis that the intercepts are jointly zero at the 5% level of significance.

The coefficient estimates inTable 5exhibit intuitive patterns that shed light on the relative success of the duration-adjusted CAPM. Panel A shows that the loadings on the equity risk factor are monotonically increasing as we move from the AA-rated to the B-rated portfolios. Panels B and C provide further support: in panel B, the duration-driven bond returns have small and statistically insignificant equity risk loadings while their duration loadings decrease monotonically from AA to B-rated bonds. In panel C, higher-rated bonds have lower exposure to the equity risk factor, consistent with their lower default risk.

Table 6reproduces the subperiod analysis fromTable 4using the duration-adjusted CAPM. Panel A shows that the duration-adjusted CAPM is significantly better than the single-factor CAPM at explaining corporate bond excess returns relative to Treasury bills.R2coefficients are substantially higher across rating categories, especially for investment-grade bonds. Panel B exhibits similar improvement for the duration-adjusted returns, especially in the 1986–2000 period when Treasury returns were positively correlated with the stock market.

Our results extend to bond characteristics other than credit rating. Because of the need for transaction data from TRACE, the illiquidity-sorted portfolio returns begin in August 2002.

We see that the duration-adjusted CAPM significantly shrinks the CAPM alphas for all the characteristics we test. For example, for the credit spread-sorted portfolios, the CAPM’s mean absoluteαof 0.25% is reduced to 0.14% when we use the duration-adjusted CAPM. The decline inαis particularly pronounced for high-quality bonds. Notably, the alpha spread between high and low portfolios is statistically insignificant for the credit spreads, downside risk, and illiquidity portfolios.

An exception to the performance of the duration-adjusted CAPM is the equity momentum portfolios. Although the duration-adjusted CAPM outperforms the CAPM, its alpha spread for the high-minus-low portfolio is highly significant at 0.45%. These results are consistent with those ofGebhardt, Hvidkjaer, and Swaminathan (2005b), who argue that equity-bond momentum is a reflection of the bond market’s underreaction to news and thus cannot be explained by systematic risk exposures. Based on our results, such an anomaly may persist in the future even if the secular decline in long-term yields is not repeated.

2.5 Comparison with existing bond pricing factors

Prior research has argued that the failure of equity market factor models to price corporate bonds is justification for separate pricing models based on bond market risk factors. Along with the steady growth of the corporate bond market, this has led to a recent proliferation of factor models with the specific goal of pricing corporate bonds (e.g.,Israel, Palhares, and Richardson 2018; Bai, Bali, and Wen 2019;Bredendiek, Ottonello, and Valkanov 2019;He, Khorrami, and Song 2022;Kelly, Palhares, and Pruitt 2023).

To assess whether this is still the case when we split out the effects of duration, we compare the performance of the duration-adjusted CAPM to the corporate bond pricing model from Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019).12The Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model has four factors: the corporate bond market return, a downside risk factor, a credit risk factor, and a liquidity risk factor.

Table 8, panel A, presents the intercepts,t-statistics, and adjustedR2coefficients from regressions of corporate bond returns on the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model, the CAPM, and the duration-adjusted CAPM. This table also considers a duration-adjusted bond market CAPM, which we describe below. As in the prior sections, we begin by using value-weighted portfolios sorted by credit rating category. The sample runs from August 2004 to December 2020 only because of the availability of the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) factors.

Similar to the full sample results inTable 3, the CAPM has poor explanatory power, especially for the investment-grade bonds. The duration-adjusted CAPM represents a significant improvement, increasing the average adjustedR2from 25% to 49%. The regression intercepts are reduced to the extent that we cannot reject that they are jointly equal to zero, which suggests that the duration-adjusted CAPM is able to price the cross-section of credit rating portfolios.

The Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model has strong explanatory power for the individual test portfolios, with adjustedR2coefficients exceeding 83% for all categories and averaging 88%. The intercepts are on average 0.05% and economically small, but the GRS test rejects the model due to the improved precision of the alpha estimates.

To assess the role of market segmentation, or the importance of including information specific to the corporate bond market in the model, we construct a duration-adjusted bond market CAPM in the same spirit as the duration-adjusted CAPM. Specifically, this is a two-factor model based on the value-weighted corporate bond market index return decomposed into its duration-matched Treasury and duration-adjusted components. The bond market index, unlike Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019), is constructed using our sample of industrial bonds. This model has substantially more explanatory power for the time series of corporate bond returns, with a minimum adjustedR2of 90% and an average of 92%. This represents an even better model fit than the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model, despite this model having only two factors instead of four, again highlighting the importance of splitting out the effects of duration. However, because this model is estimated so precisely, there are three portfolios with statistically significant intercepts (A, BB, and B), and the GRS tests reject the hypotheses that the intercepts are equal to zero or to each other. Thus, the model appears to perform nearly as well as the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model in the cross-section.

Panel C ofTable 8turns attention to the duration-adjusted corporate bond returns. In contrast to panel A, we find that the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) has higher explanatory power than the CAPM and duration-adjusted CAPM but worse the duration-adjusted bond CAPM, with an average adjustedR2that is 20 p.p. lower than the duration-adjusted bond CAPM. Moreover, the regression intercepts for the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model are larger and more statistically significant than for the CAPM and duration-adjusted CAPMs. From this perspective, the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model does not show improved performance relative to the CAPM in both the time series and the cross-section for the duration-adjusted corporate bond returns.

Consistent with the intuition discussed above, the failure of the CAPM to price corporate bonds is due to the contribution of changes in government bond yields rather than credit and liquidity risks. This suggests that the success of the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model in explaining the time series of returns is partly due to its inclusion of a corporate bond market factor and not due to its ability to price credit risk, despite the authors’ focus on default risk in the construction of the model. One practical implication of this finding is that duration-hedged investors may find the CAPM useful as a benchmark and may not benefit from using more complex models designed to price corporate bond returns.13

In this exercise, we add TERM and DEF factors of Fama and French (1993) for comparison. Table 9 reports the mean, standard deviation, and annualized Sharpe ratios of the factors we discussed above. To compare the models, Barillas et al. (2020) suggest using the bias-adjusted squared Sharpe ratio A d j . In panel A, the two factors in the duration-adjusted CAPM, the Treasury risk component and the dividend risk component, have annualized Sharpe ratios of 0.56 and –0.00, respectively. While these Sharpe ratios are not higher than those for other factors, the combination of the two factors produces a high bias-adjusted squared Sharpe ratio of 0.084 (Table 9, panel B), as the two factors are strongly negatively correlated over this sample period. This value is higher than all the models we consider in the table.

Moreover, consistent with the results above, none of the factors obtains significant alphas against the duration-adjusted CAPM, including the risk factors of Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019). For each model, we use the factors of the model as test assets and evaluate whether the alphas are jointly different from zero against the duration-adjusted CAPM. Thep-value of theGibbons, Ross, and Shanken (1989)test is 0.23 for the duration-adjusted bond CAPM, 0.76 for the model consisting ofFama and French (1993)’s TERM and DEF factors, and 0.09 for the four factors of Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019), indicating that these factors do not jointly improve the Sharpe ratio of the duration-adjusted CAPM.

For further verification, followingBarillas et al. (2020), we conduct the pairwise model comparison tests and report the results in panel B ofTable 9. The test fails to reject the null hypothesis that a model’s squared Sharpe ratio is equal to that of the other models. InTable A7in theInternet Appendix, we conduct a multiple-model comparison test. The result further demonstrates that the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model does not outperform the CAPM or its duration-adjusted variants.

In summary, the direct model comparison suggests that duration adjustment makes a significant difference in the performance of well-known risk factors and reinforces its importance in building factor models.14
article.3 Conclusion:
In this paper, we explore the importance of evaluating corporate bond returns in excess of their duration-matched government bond counterfactuals. We find that properly adjusting for duration has important implications for the empirical evaluation of pricing models for corporate bonds. In particular, we find that decomposing realized corporate bond returns into the effect of the secular decline in risk-free interest rates (as measured by the duration-matched returns on government bonds) and the effects of credit risk and liquidity results in markedly different return patterns compared to previously employed excess return definitions. In particular, while the CAPM struggles to price unadjusted investment-grade corporate bond returns, we find that it lines up quite well with duration-adjusted returns.

Our results contribute to the literature that argues for a more careful analysis of duration and its effects on ex post and ex ante return measures. We show that adjusting for the effects of duration has important implications for the measurement of risk premiums and the evaluation of asset pricing tests. This is especially important over the last four decades, a period that is used in most contemporary empirical asset pricing studies and features a steady decline in long-term interest rates that is unlikely to repeat itself in the future.

From a practitioner’s perspective, our duration-adjusted returns measure interest-rate-hedged returns on corporate bonds. Thus, the idea of separating default/liquidity risk from interest rate risk when designing investment strategies is widespread among practitioners. For example, industry-oriented bond pricing papers, such as Israel, Palhares, and Richardson (2018) and Houweling and van Zundert (2017) , adjust for duration in their analysis. Finally, Andreani, Palhares, and Richardson (2023) recommend adjusting for duration as we do, but their focus is on comparing bond databases, and they do not study the asset pricing implications of the adjustments.

7 Fixed-price call schedules allow an issuer to call the bond issue at a prespecified price, which it would typically do to refinance into debt with a lower coupon rate. Make-whole call provisions allow the issuer to call the bond at a prespecified spread to the Treasury yield curve, typically set at a low level such that it is unlikely to be exercised.

8 The quoted “clean” price of a bond is equal to the discounted value of the bond’s future cash flows, also called the “all-in” price, minus the accrued interest over the fractional coupon period between the last payment and a trade’s settlement date. In the U.S. corporate bond market, coupons are typically made on a semiannual basis and accrued interest is computed under the 30/360 daycount convention, which assumes 30 days in each month (e.g., the number of days elapsed between February 10 and March 10 is 30 days).

9 This calculation ignores the presence of embedded options in the corporate bond indenture (e.g., the issuer’s option to call the bond). Qualitatively, this results in an overstatement of a bond’s duration and the role of interest rate changes in driving its returns.

10 A similar conclusion holds for the time-series variation of returns. The Shapley-Owen decomposition of the R 2 , reported in the Internet Appendix Table A3 , indicates that a large fraction of the variation in investment-grade corporate bond returns corresponds to the duration-matched Treasury component.

11 Note that our quote data end 1 year before maturity. We confirm that yields and realized returns are equivalent for bonds that matured during the sample period if prices are interpolated through maturity.

12 We focus on the Bai, Bali, and Wen (2019) model because the factor returns are publicly available. The factor returns are not publicly available for the other models cited above.

13 In Internet Appendix Table A6 , we repeat the analysis using the earlier sample from 1976 to 1985 for investment-grade bonds. In a rising rate environment, all models perform similarly, with the duration-adjusted bond CAPM performing slightly better than other models.

14 In Internet Appendix Tables A9 and A10 , we present the results of Fama-MacBeth regressions using these factors, and find that regressions on 3-year rolling-window betas lead to insignificant estimates of the price of risk for all of the factors we tested. However, when we use portfolio betas and control for credit ratings, the price of risk for the duration factor becomes significant while the other factors remain insignificant.
